stem: An object is dropped from rest and falls freely for 3.0 s with g = 10 m/s^2. Ignoring air resistance, how far does it fall?

choices.label: A
choices.rationale: Uses one-half g t instead of one-half g t squared.

choices.label: B
choices.rationale: Computes the final speed g t and reports it as a distance.

choices.label: C
choices.rationale: Distance is one-half g t squared = 0.5 * 10 * 9 = 45 m.

choices.label: D
choices.rationale: Uses g t squared and forgets the factor of one-half.

choices.label: E
choices.rationale: Divides rather than multiplies the time factor.

solution_decomposition.subgoal: Pick the kinematics relation for start from rest.

provenance.source_ref: Synthetic Physics Notes, sec 1 Free Fall

stem: Two point charges of +2 microcoulomb each are 2.0 m apart in vacuum with k = 9e9 N*m^2/C^2. The magnitude of the electrostatic force between them is closest to

choices.label: B
choices.rationale: Divides by r instead of r squared.

choices.label: C
choices.rationale: Omits the division by r squared entirely.

choices.label: D
choices.rationale: Divides by an extra power of r.

choices.label: E
choices.rationale: Misplaces a factor of ten in the arithmetic.

provenance.source_ref: Synthetic Physics Notes, sec 12 Coulomb Law

stem: For an ideal mass-spring oscillator undergoing small oscillations, how does the period of the motion depend on the amplitude of the swing?

choices.label: A
choices.text: It is independent of the amplitude.
choices.rationale: Ideal SHM is isochronous; the period depends only on mass and stiffness.

choices.label: B
choices.rationale: Assumes a wider swing must take longer.

choices.label: C
choices.rationale: Assumes a larger swing means faster and shorter period.

choices.label: D
choices.text: It is proportional to the amplitude.
choices.rationale: Invents a linear dependence on amplitude.

choices.label: E
choices.text: It is proportional to the amplitude squared.
choices.rationale: Invents a quadratic dependence on amplitude.

solution_decomposition.rubric: T = 2 pi sqrt(m/k), no amplitude term.
Period is set by m and k alone.

provenance.source_ref: Synthetic Physics Notes, sec 6 Amplitude and Period

stem: A particle is in a one-dimensional infinite square well with ground-state energy E1. What is the energy of the n = 2 state?

choices.label: A
choices.rationale: Scales energy linearly with the level number.

choices.label: B
choices.rationale: Energies scale as n squared, so E2 = 4 E1.

choices.label: C
choices.rationale: Scales as n cubed.

choices.label: D
choices.rationale: Assumes energy falls as the level rises.

choices.label: E
choices.rationale: Squares the level number twice.

provenance.source_ref: Synthetic Physics Notes, sec 7 Particle in a Box

stem: An ideal gas is compressed isothermally to half its original volume. By what factor does its pressure change?

choices.label: A
choices.rationale: At fixed temperature pressure varies inversely with volume, so halving V doubles P.

choices.label: B
choices.rationale: Treats pressure as directly proportional to volume.

choices.label: C
choices.rationale: Assumes pressure does not respond to volume.

choices.label: D
choices.rationale: Applies an inverse-square instead of inverse relation.

choices.label: E
choices.rationale: Combines direct proportion with a squared factor.

solution_decomposition.rubric: P V = constant at fixed T.

provenance.source_ref: Synthetic Physics Notes, sec 11 Ideal Gas

stem: In a double-slit experiment with slit separation d and wavelength lambda, the angular position theta of the first bright fringe (m = 1) satisfies which condition?

choices.label: A
choices.rationale: Bright fringes obey d sin(theta) = m lambda, so m = 1 gives sin(theta) = lambda / d.

choices.label: B
choices.rationale: Inverts the ratio of wavelength to slit spacing.

choices.label: C
choices.rationale: Inserts an unwarranted factor of one-half.

choices.label: D
choices.rationale: Uses the dark-fringe (minimum) condition by mistake.

choices.label: E
choices.rationale: Swaps wavelength and slit spacing in the relation.

provenance.source_ref: Synthetic Physics Notes, sec 10 Double Slit
